hook: Australia's first animated neon sign, a vinegar girl forever skipping
facts: Erected 1936 to advertise Skipping Girl Vinegar, possibly the country's first animated neon sign
Nicknamed 'Little Audrey'; outlines flick through four rope positions to fake motion
Removed in 1968, then rebuilt on a nearby roof in 1970 after public outcry
Victorian Heritage listed and restored in 2009
w: Locals call her Little Audrey, the neon girl who has skipped above Victoria Street since 1936, flicking through four rope positions to fake the motion. Possibly Australia's first animated sign, she once sold vinegar. Torn down in 1968, she came back in 1970 after the city refused to let her go. Forever mid-jump.

hook: 1930s bachelor flats built on the radical idea of minimum space, minimum rent
facts: Designed 1935 by modernist Acheson Best Overend, completed 1936
28 mostly studio apartments in U-shaped blue clinker brick with curved balconies
Built explicitly to house bachelors with fold-out furniture and service hatchways
Heritage-listed; came with communal dining, laundry and meal service
w: Twenty-eight tiny studios in curved clinker brick, drawn up in 1935 around one radical pitch: maximum comfort in minimum space at minimum rent. Best Overend kitted the bachelor flats with fold-out furniture, service hatchways and a shared dining room, importing Europe's 'minimum flat' creed to Fitzroy. Living small, decades early.

hook: A Belfast tall ship saved from the scrapyard for one cent
facts: Iron-hulled three-masted barque launched in Belfast in 1885
Made 17 trips around the world hauling coal, nitrate and wheat before becoming a coal hulk
Handed to the National Trust in 1968 for the price of one cent
Restored over roughly 60,000 volunteer hours; won a World Ship Trust award in 1988
w: Launched in Belfast in 1885 and named for the owner's wife, this iron barque rounded the world 17 times hauling coal and nitrate before ending up a grubby coal hulk in Melbourne. The National Trust took her in 1968 for a single cent, then volunteers poured 60,000 hours into the rigging. A scrap-heap survivor moored in a dry dock.

hook: A Victorian lead-shot factory trapped under a giant glass cone
facts: Brick tower built 1888-1889 by lead manufacturer Walter Coop
Molten lead dropped down its height formed into spherical shot in a water vat below
Produced around six tonnes of shot a week until 1961
Enclosed in 1991 under an 84-metre glass cone designed by Kisho Kurokawa
w: From 1889 workers dropped molten lead down this brick tower; by the time it hit the water vat below it had pulled itself into perfect spheres of shot, six tonnes a week until 1961. When developers couldn't demolish it, they built a shopping mall around it and capped the lot with an 84-metre glass cone. Antiquity under glass.

hook: A Yorkshire cottage shipped to Melbourne brick by brick in 253 crates
facts: Built 1755 in Great Ayton, Yorkshire, by the parents of Captain James Cook
Bought and shipped to Melbourne in 1934 by philanthropist Russell Grimwade
Dismantled into 253 cases and 40 barrels for the voyage from Hull
There is no firm proof Cook himself ever lived in it
legend: Often sold as Cook's own home, though he may never have set foot inside
w: Built in Yorkshire in 1755 by Captain Cook's parents, this little stone house was bought in 1934 by a Melbourne philanthropist, taken apart brick by brick into 253 crates and 40 barrels, and reassembled in Fitzroy Gardens. The catch: Cook, already at sea by then, probably never lived here. A souvenir cottage with an apostrophe problem.

hook: A 300-year-old red gum stump carved with fairies and gnomes for the city's children
facts: Sculptor Ola Cohn carved the dead red gum stump between 1931 and 1934
Covered in fairies, dwarfs, gnomes, kookaburras and Australian animals
Cohn donated it 'for the children of Melbourne and especially the fairies'
Chemically preserved in the 1970s and set on a concrete base
legend: Said to be planted by fairies; children leave offerings at its base
w: Over three years from 1931, sculptor Ola Cohn chiselled fairies, gnomes, dwarfs and kookaburras into the stump of an ancient red gum, then gave it 'to the children of Melbourne and especially the fairies'. Rot forced a chemical rescue in the 1970s. Kids still leave small offerings in its hollows. Folklore carved into a dead tree.

hook: The cafe that gave Melbourne its first real espresso, and lost its much-loved owner
facts: Opened 1954 by migrant brothers Leo and Vildo Pellegrini in a former tailor's shop
Among the first Melbourne cafes to run an authentic Italian espresso machine
Co-owner Sisto Malaspina killed in the 2018 Bourke Street attack; mourned citywide
National Trust listed in 2014; still run by the Malaspina family
w: When the Pellegrini brothers wired up an Italian espresso machine in this old tailor's shop in 1954, they more or less invented Melbourne's coffee habit. The mosaic-floored bar barely changed in seventy years. When co-owner Sisto Malaspina died in the 2018 Bourke Street attack, the whole city queued to grieve. Still pouring regardless.

hook: A bluestone prison hung with plaster casts of executed faces
facts: Operated as a gaol 1845-1924; 133 people hanged inside
Holds one of Australia's largest collections of prisoner death masks
Plaster casts were taken so phrenologists could read 'the criminal mind'
Displays Ned Kelly's death mask, pistol and replica armour
legend: Visitors report disembodied voices around the cellblock
w: Inside this bluestone cellblock, where 133 people were hanged between 1845 and 1924, the walls carry plaster casts of the dead. Phrenologists once took these death masks to read criminality in a brow's curve. Ned Kelly's face hangs among them, beside his pistol. They say voices still drift through the tiers. Pseudoscience in plaster.

hook: A dead-end service lane papered floor to ceiling in tiny framed artworks
facts: Covered laneway off Howey Place, near Little Collins Street
Began when artists glued cheap framed European prints to the brick wall
Grew by accumulation; the lane has never been buffed clean
Featured in ABC's Not Quite Art in 2007
w: Plain from the mouth, this dead-end service lane off Howey Place hides a brick wall crammed with hundreds of tiny framed artworks. It started when someone glued cheap European prints in frames to the bricks; the prints rotted, the frames stayed, and artists kept filling them. Never once buffed clean. A gallery that grew by accretion.

hook: A working cabinet of curiosities selling fossils, skulls and meteorites
facts: Trades from a heritage building at 439 Lonsdale Street
Stocks fossils, minerals, taxidermy, skeletons, antique maps and globes
Name borrows the Renaissance term for a curiosity cabinet
Blurs the line between museum and shop; everything carries a price tag
w: Behind a heritage shopfront on Lonsdale Street sits a true Wunderkammer, the old word for a curiosity cabinet: fossils, meteorites, mounted beetles, skeletons, antique globes and skulls crowding every shelf. It looks like a natural history museum, except the labels carry price tags. A cabinet of wonders you can buy by the piece.

hook: The world's oldest union building, still pocked from a 1915 shootout
facts: Victorian Trades Hall opened on the site in 1859; among the oldest labour buildings on earth
In October 1915 a constable heard burglars drilling a safe inside
The raid ended in a gun battle on the main staircase
Bullet holes from the 'Great Trades Hall Robbery' remain in the walls
w: One of the oldest union buildings on earth carries a stranger scar. At 2am in October 1915, a constable passing the darkened hall heard tapping inside: burglars drilling the safe. The raid ended in a running gun battle up the main staircase. Look closely and the bullet holes still freckle the plaster. Labour history written in lead.

hook: A grotto to the King of Rock 'n' Roll, who is buried in Tennessee
facts: Built in Melbourne General Cemetery by the Elvis Presley Fan Club of Victoria
Erected 26 November 1977, just months after Presley's death
Personally approved by Vernon Presley, the singer's father
Among the cemetery's largest memorials, set in a landscaped stone grotto
w: Among the Victorian graves of a Carlton North cemetery stands a stone grotto to a man buried 15,000 kilometres away in Memphis. Grieving fans built this Elvis shrine in 1977, months after his death, and got the blessing of his father Vernon. For years they held candlelit vigils here. A cenotaph for the King, far from Graceland.
